provenance.source_corpus: The Regeneration of a Highly Disturbed Ecosystem: A Mined Peatland in Southern Québec

source_spans.text: The Cacouna peatland lost 34% of its initial peat volume between 1946 and 1998, mainly as a result of peat mining, peat subsidence, and agricultural activities.
The Cacouna peatland lost 34% of its initial peat volume between 1946 and 1998, mainly as a result of peat mining, peat subsidence, and agricultural activities.
More than 60% of the total area of the bog was mined between 1942 and 1975, and 98 km of ditches were dug to drain the site.
More than 60% of the total area of the bog was mined between 1942 and 1975, and 98 km of ditches were dug to drain the site.
During the mining period, drainage ditches probably drew off a large quantity of water from the peatland.
high water table level... appears to be the most important abiotic factor facilitating the establishment and survival of Sphagnum colonies.
high water table level... appears to be the most important abiotic factor facilitating the establishment and survival of Sphagnum colonies.
Tree species are more abundant in drier sites.
The intensity of mining activities and the pattern of abandonment of mined sectors strongly influenced abiotic factors, which in turn affected the revegetation process.
The intensity of mining activities and the pattern of abandonment of mined sectors strongly influenced abiotic factors, which in turn affected the revegetation process.
The absence of a widespread Sphagnum cover, a keystone plant in a bog environment... suggests that the fundamental carbon accumulation function has not been restored
